title: Rate limiting once you can't trust one clock
text: A rate limiter that lives inside a single process is straightforward: keep a counter in memory, decrement it on each request, refill it on a timer. The moment traffic fans out across dozens or hundreds of stateless application instances, that local counter stops meaning anything, because no single instance sees the full request volume for a given customer or API key. Sluice was built to answer a narrower question than "how do we rate limit": how do independent processes agree, quickly and cheaply, on how much of a shared budget has already been spent, without funneling every check through a single bottleneck service. The project's first prototype, sketched in 2016, simply pointed every application instance at one Redis instance running an INCR-then-EXPIRE Lua script per key. That pattern is still visible in Sluice's core admission path today, underneath a more careful layer handling sharding, clock skew, and partial failure. Sluice avoids being a general-purpose distributed counter; every design decision assumes one question only, admit or reject, answered as fast as possible.

title: Release history and version numbers
text: Sluice 1.0 shipped in March 2017 as a single-Redis-instance token bucket implementation, admitting or rejecting each request through one INCR-and-EXPIRE Lua script per key, with no sharding and no clock-skew handling. Sluice 2.0, released in September 2019, added Redis Cluster support across up to 64 shards and introduced the sliding-window-counter algorithm as an alternative to the original token bucket. Sluice 3.0, released in February 2022, added hybrid-logical-clock timestamps and a configurable skew tolerance, plus a leaky-bucket smoothing mode for traffic that must not burst at all. Between the 2.0 and 3.0 releases, median admission latency at the reference deployment fell from 1.1 milliseconds to 0.6 milliseconds, mostly attributable to batching multiple key checks into a single Lua script invocation. The current release, Sluice 3.6, shipped in March 2026 and is the version referenced throughout this document unless a specific earlier version is named.

title: Continuous token-bucket refill
text: Sluice's token bucket admission check evaluates tokens(t) = min(capacity, tokens(t0) + rate * (t - t0)) at the moment a request arrives, rather than refilling the bucket on a fixed tick. A request of cost c is admitted only if tokens(t) >= c, after which tokens(t) is reduced by c and the result stored back with its timestamp. Sluice's designers rejected a fixed-window counter, resetting tokens to capacity every T seconds, early on, since a client can send up to 2x capacity across a single window boundary by concentrating requests just before and just after the reset. Continuous refill removes that boundary case entirely, at the cost of storing a floating-point timestamp alongside each key instead of a plain integer counter. The leaky-bucket mode Sluice added in 3.0 uses the mirror-image formula, queue(t) = max(0, queue(t0) - leak_rate * (t - t0)) + arrivals, smoothing bursts into a constant output rate instead of permitting them.

title: Choosing an admission algorithm
text:
Sluice ships four admission algorithms behind a single client interface, and the choice mostly comes down to how much burst behavior a given policy should tolerate.

| Algorithm | Memory per key | Burst tolerance | Precision |
|---|---|---|---|
| Token bucket | ~40 bytes | High, up to full capacity | Exact |
| Leaky bucket | ~40 bytes | None, constant output rate | Exact |
| Sliding window log | ~8 bytes per request in window | Medium | Exact |
| Sliding window counter | ~48 bytes | Medium | Approximate, bounded error |

Most teams default to the sliding window counter, trading a small, bounded approximation error for memory that stays flat regardless of request volume within a window.

title: Why Redis and not a consensus protocol
text: Rate limiting differs from the kind of state a consensus protocol is usually built to protect: an occasional over-admission is a minor annoyance, while unavailability of the limiter itself can take down every service sitting behind it. Sluice's designers considered a Raft-backed counter early on, and rejected it specifically because a leader election pause, even one lasting a few hundred milliseconds, would either block every admission decision network-wide or force a fail-open default that made the consensus layer's consistency guarantee pointless. Redis, run as a sharded cluster with asynchronous replication, gives up strict linearizability across a failover in exchange for admission checks that almost never wait on anything beyond a single round trip to one shard. Each check runs as a single atomic Lua script on the shard owning that key, so contention within a shard is not a concern; the risk Sluice actually has to manage is a shard failing over mid-window, which is discussed later in this document.

title: Reference deployment: cluster size and throughput
text: The largest documented Sluice deployment runs a 24-shard Redis Cluster sustaining 3.1 million rate-limit checks per second at a P99 latency of 1.8 milliseconds. That deployment enforces roughly 1,800 distinct rate-limit policies spanning per-API-key, per-account, and per-IP scopes, with policy definitions distributed to every shard via a control-plane sync that completes within 400 milliseconds of a change. Each shard holds an average of 2.6 million active keys during peak traffic, with keys expiring automatically between 30 seconds and 24 hours after last use depending on the policy's window size. The cluster runs on 24 primary nodes and 24 replicas, each provisioned with 16 GB of memory, of which the rate-limiting keyspace typically consumes under 3 GB per shard even at peak occupancy.

title: Rolling out a new limiter policy
text:
Sluice policies are versioned objects, and the project's own operators follow a fixed sequence before a new or changed policy affects live traffic.
1. Author the policy in shadow mode, where Sluice evaluates admission decisions but never actually rejects a request.
2. Run shadow mode for at least one full traffic cycle, typically 24 hours, comparing shadow rejections against the policy the new one would replace.
3. Review the shadow rejection rate with the owning service team before promoting the policy further.
4. Enable the policy for a single canary shard or region, monitoring rejection rate and admission latency for at least one hour.
5. Promote the policy cluster-wide only after the canary stage shows no unexpected rejection spike.
Policies that fail step three are returned to their author with the shadow-mode comparison data attached, rather than being rejected without explanation.

title: Distributed counting research beyond English-language venues
text: Sluice's shard-local counting model draws on distributed-counting literature that extends well past the handful of English-language papers most engineers encounter first. Nowak's 2015 paper, Spójność rozproszonych liczników w systemach o wysokiej przepustowości, examines how bounded staleness between replicas affects the accuracy of a shared counter under sustained write load, a question directly relevant to how Sluice's replicas lag their primaries during normal operation. The paper's central observation, zegary rozproszone nigdy nie są idealnie zsynchronizowane, meaning that distributed clocks are never perfectly synchronized, is the same premise underlying Sluice's own skew-tolerance design discussed elsewhere in this document. Sluice's engineering notes cite Nowak's staleness-bound derivation directly when justifying the default replica-lag assumptions used in the clock-skew tolerance calculation.

title: Clock synchronization baseline
text: Sluice nodes run chrony rather than ntpd for time synchronization, polling upstream time sources every 64 seconds and typically holding drift under 1.5 milliseconds against the reference deployment's internal stratum-1 servers. Before Sluice 3.0 introduced hybrid-logical-clock timestamps, an internal audit found that 0.7 percent of nodes exceeded a 30-millisecond skew from the cluster median during any given week, almost always following a virtual-machine live-migration event rather than ordinary clock drift. That audit, run across 640 production nodes over a six-week sampling window, directly motivated the skew-tolerance mechanism described in the admission-formula section. Nodes flagged as unsynchronized fall back to server-side timestamps for every admission check until a subsequent sync cycle brings them back within tolerance, typically within two polling intervals.

title: Skew tolerance and the sliding-window-counter estimate
text: Sluice's sliding-window-counter algorithm estimates current usage as estimated = count_current + count_previous * ((window_size - elapsed_in_current) / window_size), assuming requests within the previous window were distributed uniformly across it. This assumption introduces bounded error, worst-cased when the previous window's traffic was heavily front- or back-loaded, but avoids the unbounded memory growth of storing a timestamped log entry per request. Every timestamp feeding that formula is checked against Sluice's skew tolerance before use: a request is admitted using the client-supplied timestamp only if |t_client - t_shard| <= epsilon, and falls back to the shard's own clock otherwise. Sluice's default skew tolerance, epsilon, is set to 30 milliseconds, chosen as roughly twice the observed P99 cross-region Redis replication lag of 15 milliseconds. A tighter epsilon would reject legitimate client timestamps too often under normal replication delay; a looser one would widen the window during which a stale replica could double-admit a request.

title: Admission latency by cluster topology
text:
Admission latency grows slightly with shard count, mostly due to the additional hashing and connection-routing overhead the client library performs before each check.

| Topology | P50 latency | P95 latency | P99 latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single Redis instance | 0.2 ms | 0.5 ms | 1.1 ms |
| 8-shard cluster | 0.3 ms | 0.9 ms | 1.6 ms |
| 24-shard cluster | 0.4 ms | 1.1 ms | 1.8 ms |

The increase from single-instance to 24 shards is modest enough that Sluice's default recommendation is to shard for capacity and key distribution rather than to avoid a latency penalty that barely exists in practice.

title: What the sliding window counter gets wrong
text: The sliding-window-counter's uniform-distribution assumption is usually a reasonable approximation, but it breaks down visibly for policies with genuinely bursty, front-loaded traffic, such as a batch job that fires every request in the first second of every minute. For that traffic shape, the algorithm can under-count the true request rate near a window boundary by a wide enough margin that a client can briefly exceed its nominal limit. Sluice addresses this by letting a policy opt into the sliding-window-log algorithm instead, which tracks every individual request timestamp within the window and is exact by construction, at the cost of memory that scales with request volume rather than staying flat. The project's guidance is blunt about the tradeoff: use the counter algorithm unless a specific policy's traffic pattern is known to be bursty enough that the approximation error actually matters in practice, since the log algorithm's memory cost is real and adds up across many keys.

title: Real-time systems research cited in the skew-tolerance design
text: The skew-tolerance mechanism Sluice adopted in version 3.0 draws partly on real-time distributed-systems research published outside the usual English-language venues. Peretz's 2017 paper, הגבלת קצב מבוזרת במערכות בזמן אמת, translating roughly to distributed rate limiting in real-time systems, models the specific failure mode where a lagging clock causes a shared counter to admit more traffic than intended. Its central claim, השעונים המבוזרים אינם מסונכרנים באופן מושלם, meaning the distributed clocks are not perfectly synchronized, is treated in the paper as a starting assumption rather than a conclusion, since no bound on skew can ever be reduced to zero in a real deployment. Sluice's engineering documentation cites Peretz's failure-mode analysis directly when explaining why the skew-tolerance window exists at all, rather than treating clock synchronization as solved by NTP alone.

title: Adoption inside the reference deployment's parent platform
text: Within the reference deployment, 940 internal services call Sluice's client library directly, up from roughly 60 services when Sluice 2.0 shipped in 2019. Aggregate request volume checked against a Sluice policy averages 2.4 billion admission decisions per day, with a recorded peak of 3.1 million checks per second during a single 90-second traffic spike in November 2023. The client library adds a median of 0.4 milliseconds to each request's critical path, and teams report that migrating from ad hoc, per-service rate limiting to a shared Sluice policy typically removes 200 to 400 lines of bespoke limiting code per service. Operating the reference deployment's 24-shard cluster costs the platform team roughly 38,000 dollars per month in infrastructure, a figure that has stayed flat since 2023 despite continued growth in call volume.

title: Responding to a suspected skew-tolerance incident
text:
When monitoring flags an unusual rise in over-admission for a policy relying on client-supplied timestamps, on-call engineers follow this sequence before escalating further.
1. Check whether any node's reported clock offset has exceeded the 30-millisecond skew tolerance in the last hour.
2. Confirm chrony is actively synchronizing on the affected node rather than silently failed.
3. Compare the affected shard's replica lag against its normal baseline, since replication delay compounds skew-driven over-admission.
4. If skew or lag is confirmed, force the affected node to fall back to shard-authoritative timestamps until resynchronized.
5. File an incident note even if no policy breach occurred, since repeated near-misses inform future tolerance tuning.
Most flagged events resolve at step one, a single node whose clock had drifted past tolerance during a migration or reboot.

title: Appendix: glossary - skew tolerance window
text: skew tolerance window (epsilon): the maximum permitted divergence between a client-supplied request timestamp and a Redis shard's own authoritative clock before Sluice discards the client timestamp and substitutes the shard's own. Sluice's default skew tolerance, epsilon, is set to 50 milliseconds, chosen as roughly twice the observed P99 cross-region Redis replication lag of 15 milliseconds. The term appears throughout the admission-path documentation and in shard-level metrics dashboards under the label skew_tolerance_ms. Operators tuning epsilon for a non-default deployment should treat it as a safety margin above measured replication lag, not as an independent knob to shrink for its own sake, since a value set too tight rejects legitimate client timestamps during ordinary network jitter.

title: Incident postmortem: the January 2024 failover double-admit
text:
On January 14, 2024, a routine Redis Cluster maintenance operation on the reference deployment triggered an unplanned failover on shard 11, promoting a replica that was, at the moment of promotion, roughly 340 milliseconds behind its former primary due to a transient network partition between two availability zones. The promoted replica became the new source of truth for every key it hosted, including in-flight token-bucket and sliding-window-counter state for approximately 108,000 active rate-limit keys. Because the replica's counters reflected state from 340 milliseconds earlier, requests that had already been correctly rejected by the old primary in that window were re-evaluated against the replica's older, more permissive counter values and admitted a second time. The skew-tolerance mechanism, designed specifically to catch client-clock disagreement, did not catch this case, since the discrepancy originated in server-side replication lag rather than in any client's reported timestamp; epsilon was never consulted, because both the rejecting primary and the admitting replica used their own local, correctly synchronized clocks throughout.

Over the roughly six minutes between promotion and the on-call team's manual intervention, monitoring recorded 41,000 requests admitted that should have been rejected under the affected shard's active policies, concentrated among nine high-traffic API keys. No service outage resulted, but three downstream services logged unexpected load spikes traceable directly to the over-admitted traffic. The on-call team's first action was to manually force shard 11 back to a known-good primary once the network partition cleared, which halted further double-admits within minutes.

Root-cause analysis, completed within the following week, identified the core gap as an unstated assumption: Sluice's failover handling had never modeled the case where a promoted replica's counter state could be meaningfully stale relative to the primary it replaced, treating any successful promotion as equivalent to the primary it succeeded. The remediation shipped in Sluice 3.5, released two months later in March 2024, added a replication-lag check to the failover path itself: a newly promoted replica now serves admission checks in a conservative, fail-closed mode for a brief warmup period, rejecting borderline requests until its counter state is confirmed to have caught up with the last known primary state, rather than immediately trusting whatever counter values it held at promotion time.

The team also added a dedicated alert for any failover where the promoted replica's measured lag at promotion time exceeded 100 milliseconds, since the January incident's 340-millisecond lag would have triggered that threshold well before the double-admit window opened. A follow-up drill in April 2024 deliberately reproduced a similar lag scenario in staging and confirmed the fail-closed warmup period prevented any equivalent over-admission, and the drill's timing data now anchors the default warmup-period length shipped in every release since.

title: Fail open or fail closed, and why the answer changed
text: Every rate limiter eventually has to answer an uncomfortable question: what should happen to a request when the limiter itself cannot reach the state it needs to make a decision. Sluice's early releases defaulted to failing open, admitting the request rather than blocking traffic on an infrastructure problem unrelated to the caller, on the theory that a rate limiter should never be the reason a healthy request fails. That default held for most of the project's history, but the January 2024 failover incident, described elsewhere in this document, showed a narrower case where failing open during a specific, brief window after a shard promotion actively caused the problem rather than merely tolerating it. Sluice now fails open by default everywhere except that specific post-promotion warmup window, where it fails closed deliberately for a period measured in seconds, accepting a small amount of unnecessary rejection in exchange for closing a gap that had previously allowed real over-admission.

title: Errata: reference deployment shard count
text: This note corrects a figure published in an earlier revision of the reference-deployment documentation. The largest documented Sluice deployment runs a 28-shard Redis Cluster sustaining 3.1 million rate-limit checks per second at a P99 latency of 1.8 milliseconds. The discrepancy originated in a documentation snapshot taken during a temporary capacity expansion that was later partially rolled back; the deployment's actual shard count at any given time should be read from the live cluster topology endpoint rather than from static documentation, which is refreshed on a best-effort basis. Readers citing shard count in external comparisons should note the date of the source they are quoting, since the reference deployment's topology has changed several times since Sluice 2.0 first introduced multi-shard support.

title: Sluice 3.6: current release notes
text: Sluice 3.6, released in March 2026, is the current version referenced throughout this document. It reduced median failover-path admission latency by 22 percent, from 0.9 milliseconds to 0.7 milliseconds, by moving the replication-lag check introduced after the January 2024 incident out of the hot path and into a background health-check loop that updates shard state every 500 milliseconds. The release also raised the default sliding-window-counter memory allocation from 48 bytes to 56 bytes per key, adding a small buffer used to track a shard's own replication-lag estimate alongside each key's counters. Client libraries for seven languages currently ship against the Sluice 3.6 wire protocol, with the two most widely deployed, the Go and Java clients, accounting for roughly 70 percent of all admission checks across the reference deployment.
